TCS just cut fresher hiring by 43% — and the real story is bigger than the number.

India’s largest IT company has extended only 25,000 job offers to fresh graduates for FY27 — down sharply from 44,000 in FY26, which was itself one of the highest fresher intakes in the Indian private sector that year.

CEO K Krithivasan confirmed the numbers and was direct: more hiring will happen only when demand clarity improves.

Here’s what’s actually driving this shift:

→ TCS’s total headcount fell by ~24,000 in FY26, even as it hired 44,000 freshers — meaning attrition and layoffs were running parallel to campus hiring
→ 12,000 senior-level roles were cut in FY26, driven by a structural shift in how projects are executed — not AI, as Krithivasan explicitly clarified
→ Freshers need up to 9 months of training before becoming billable; laterals contribute immediately — making fresher hiring a long-term bet that depends on future demand visibility
→ TCS now operates on a demand-driven hiring model, not a talent-pooling one

The broader context: a growing share of entry-level IT tasks — basic coding, testing, debugging — is increasingly being handled by AI tools. Large IT firms are recalibrating what “entry-level” even means in this environment.

For India’s engineering graduates, this is a signal worth reading carefully.

TCS still booked $40 billion in total contract value in FY26. The business is stable. But the old model of mass campus hiring as a default strategy is clearly changing.

What does this mean for the next generation of tech talent in India?

GenZ wants to work remotely

The Millennial wants to work onsite

Hiring Managers want to get “the best person for the job”

And somewhere in the middle… the workplace is quietly having an identity crisis.

Because let’s be honest—this is no longer just about where we work.

It’s about how we define work itself.

A few years ago, the “ideal job” looked predictable:
9–5, physical office, clear hierarchy, slow growth ladder.

Today? That model feels almost… outdated.


Now we have:

👉Remote-first roles that prioritize output over presence

👉Hybrid setups trying (and often struggling) to balance flexibility and control

👉Contract and freelance roles replacing “job security” with “income diversity”

👉Creator-led careers where people build personal brands instead of climbing corporate ladders

👉AI-augmented jobs where your biggest competition isn’t another human—it’s efficiency

So when Gen Z says they want remote work, it’s not just about comfort.

It’s about autonomy. Control. A rejection of rigid systems.

When Millennials push for onsite or hybrid, it’s not outdated thinking.

It’s structure. Collaboration. Stability in a world that’s constantly shifting.

And when hiring managers say they want “the best person for the job,”
what they often mean is:

👉 Someone who can deliver results
👉 Adapt quickly
👉 Communicate clearly
👉 And fit into a system that is still evolving

Here’s the uncomfortable truth most people won’t say out loud:

The job market is no longer designed to make everyone comfortable.

It’s designed to reward:

👉Adaptability over loyalty
👉Skills over tenure
👉Value creation over time spent

So the real question is not:
“Remote or onsite?”
It’s:
👉 Can you thrive in uncertainty?
👉 Can you prove your value regardless of location?
👉 Can you evolve faster than the job market itself?

Because the future of work isn’t one model replacing another.

It’s a messy, unpredictable mix of all of them

And the people who win won’t be the ones arguing about where to work…

They’ll be the ones who understand how to stay relevant, anywhere.

Curious—where do you stand right now? Remote, onsite, or somewhere in between?

The core message is a single, punchy truth that every QA/SDET on LinkedIn feels right now: QA is no longer just testing. AI hasn't replaced quality engineers — it has fundamentally elevated what the job demands. The poster lands that message fast, then backs it with data and trends that give readers something concrete to react to.

The 3 headline stats were chosen deliberately. The 70%+ developer stat (developers routinely rewrite AI-generated code before it's production-ready) is counterintuitive and attention-grabbing — it positions QA as the accountability layer that AI cannot replace. "Self-Healing" and "Agentic" anchor the two biggest tooling shifts happening right now, giving mid-career QA engineers a vocabulary upgrade they can use in their own interviews and LinkedIn conversations.

The 6 trends are grounded in what industry sources — Parasoft, Tricentis, Xray, ACCELQ — are reporting for 2026. They are not generic. Each one maps directly to a skill gap or a hiring signal in the SDET/automation market:
Agentic AI Testing — AI-driven test agents (Tricentis, Testim, etc.) are actively replacing manual trigger-based pipelines. Companies hiring SDET now ask about this in first-round interviews.

Self-Healing Frameworks — Playwright and Cypress are adding AI locator healing natively. This is no longer a premium feature — it's becoming table stakes in 2026 job descriptions.

AI-Code Validation — More than 50% of AI-generated code samples contain logical or security flaws. QA engineers are being asked to build "AI checklists" as a formal process. This is a brand new niche with almost no competition yet.
Shift-Left + Shift-Right — Testing has escaped the QA silo and now runs at every pipeline stage: static analysis at commit, API contract tests at build, real-user observability in production. SDETs who understand this full spectrum command 30–40% higher salaries.

Quality Observability — Production data is feeding back into test coverage decisions. This is the bridge between QA and SRE — a convergence that creates a premium new role profile.

AI Bias & Ethics Testing — As AI systems enter healthcare, finance, and education (your own education platform work is relevant here), QA must now validate that AI doesn't exhibit bias or unsafe behavior. Barely any QA professionals are skilled here yet — massive first-mover advantage.

The shekel hit its strongest in 30 years / USD last week. Will it kill tech exporter margins and threaten jobs in Israel? 

From the Weekend Edition - brought to you by TV10 Israel

Israel continues to attract formidable cross-border capital flow, evidenced this week by Palo Alto Networks’ strategic acquisition of Israeli AI-defender Koi, and EagleNXT’s $10 million equity injection into defense-drone manufacturer ThirdEye Systems. Yet, beneath these headline-grabbing transactions, the day-to-day operational reality for Israeli exporters is deteriorating due to a severe currency mismatch.

Global tech contracts and M&A valuations are universally denominated in US dollars. To understand the sheer scale of this currency drag, look at the recent M&A market. When Google acquired Wiz for $32 billion on March 18, 2025, that capital injection was worth roughly ₪115 billion to the local ecosystem. Today, at a sub-3.00 exchange rate, that exact same $32 billion valuation yields just ₪95 billion. That is a staggering ₪20 billion, a 17.39% contraction, in local purchasing power wiped out by FX mechanics alone.

While repatriated top-line revenue shrinks, operational expenditures remain firmly anchored in the expensive shekel. For US-based multinationals operating massive R&D hubs in Israel, the math is becoming untenable. 

These tech giants fund their global operations in dollars but pay their local workforce in shekels. With the exchange rate dropping below 3.00, the dollar-cost of maintaining those Israeli payrolls just spiked by tens of percentage points. Given that Tel Aviv software engineers are already among the most expensive in the world, this severe currency shock threatens to erase the cost-benefit of hiring locally.

If the USD/ILS pair remains depressed, multinational tech firms and local unicorns may accelerate the offshoring of R&D headcount to cheaper jurisdictions to protect their margins. 

If the threat of multinational hiring freezes or capital flight becomes severe enough, it could be the ultimate catalyst that forces the Bank of Israel’s hand, compelling the central bank to cut interest rates to devalue the shekel and save the tech sector, even at the risk of letting domestic inflation run hot.

Contract vs Perm in Project Services: What’s actually shifting in Melbourne right now? 

We’re seeing a clear reset in how businesses are hiring across transformation and change.

It’s no longer contract vs perm, it’s about when and why.
Right now:
Contract is being used surgically → to deliver critical phases, unblock programs, and bring in niche capability fast 
Perm is being used strategically → to build long-term transformation capability and embed change into the business 

And here’s the shift most aren’t talking about 👇
A lot of organisations are holding perm hiring early, leaning on contract to get moving… then converting once direction is clearer.
Why?
Because transformation itself is changing.
We’re moving from “projects” → to continuous change, heavily influenced by AI, automation, and evolving operating models.
That means:
Shorter, sharper contracts (6–12 months is the norm) 
Greater demand for specialists who can hit the ground running 
And a stronger focus on people who can lead change, not just deliver tasks 

From where I sit, the businesses getting ahead aren’t choosing one model — they’re blending both, deliberately.
Because in this market, flexibility wins.
But clarity on outcomes wins more.

He counts AI as employees.

Not tools. Not software. Employees.

And they're hiring humans who can work WITH those agents — not humans who compete against them.

This changes everything about what a 'good candidate' looks like.

The student who memorised case frameworks?
Might lose to the one who asked better questions to Lilli.

The analyst who worked 80-hour weeks?
Might be replaced by someone who runs 10 agents in parallel and reviews the output.

This is not a warning. It's a signal.

The firms rewriting the rules aren't waiting for regulations or roadmaps.
They're already operating in the new world.

The question is — are you?

Drop your thoughts below. Especially if you're a student or early-career professional — how are you thinking about this? 👇

#AI #McKinsey #FutureOfWork #AIAgents #CareerGrowth #ArtificialIntelligence #Innovation #TechIndia
